title: When you rename or delete a tagged test, retire its orphan — or it pins the AC red forever
when_to_read: Right after you rename, move, or delete a test that was tagged to an AC (tagAc), and any time an AC reads `failing`/`stale` but you believe the live tests pass. The actor who changed the test is the only one who knows its old identifier is dead — retiring it is your move, not a human's later cleanup.
body:
## The failure mode

Verification is keyed by `test_identifier` (`${file}::${test name}`). The verdict reads the `test_event_latest` summary (one row per `(ac_uid, test_identifier)`); an AC is `failing` if any identifier's latest emission is `fail`/`error`.

When you **rename, move, or delete** a tagged test, its old `test_identifier` stops emitting — but its last emission stays in the summary forever. If that last emission was a `fail`, the AC stays red even though the live suite is green. The dead identifier is an **orphan**: it names a test that no longer exists in the codebase, yet it's indistinguishable from a real, actively-failing test. (The motivating incident: spec-118 ac-7 was split from one combined test into three; the original combined identifier's last `fail` pinned ac-7 red for days. Same shape hit spec-100 when its gutter tests were renamed.)

## Why the server can't fix this for you

The server cannot tell "this test was renamed away" from "this test failed for real and just wasn't re-run this round". Auto-retiring on absence would false-green an AC whose test genuinely failed but was merely skipped in a partial / filtered / sharded run — a stale GREEN hiding a real failure, strictly worse than a stale red. So automatic detection is **deferred**. But **you** — the actor who just renamed or deleted the test — have perfect knowledge of which identifier is dead. Retirement is therefore explicit and actor-driven.

## The fix: retire the orphan (soft, reversible)

Retirement is a **soft hide**, not a delete. It sets `hidden=true` on the dead identifier's emissions (audit trail preserved) and drops them from the verification badge. It is fully reversible, and **self-healing**: if you retire the wrong identifier, a later live emission of that same identifier lands `hidden=false` and re-enters the verdict on its next run — so a mistaken hide can never leave a durable false green.

Three ref-keyed tools, all addressed by the canonical AC ref (no UUID lookup):

1. `get_test_matrix(ref)` — inspect. One row per `test_identifier`: latest status, emission count, and flags. `PINNING red` = this identifier holds the AC red right now. `retired (hidden)` = already soft-hidden.
2. `discontinue_test_events(ref, test_identifier)` — retire an orphan you KNOW is gone from the codebase. Soft, reversible.
3. `restore_test_events(ref, test_identifier)` — un-hide one you retired by mistake (the test still exists); recomputes the badge from the restored history.

## The trigger (make this a habit)

**After you rename, move, or delete a test tagged to an AC, check that AC and retire the orphan.** Concretely:

1. You changed `tests/foo.test.ts::old name` → it no longer emits under that identifier.
2. `get_ac(ref)` / `list_acs(ref)` now flags the AC as failing, pinned by `"tests/foo.test.ts::old name"`.
3. Confirm with `get_test_matrix(ref)` that the pinning identifier is the one you removed.
4. `discontinue_test_events(ref, "tests/foo.test.ts::old name")`. The AC's verdict clears.

Only retire an identifier you are sure no longer exists in the codebase. If you're unsure whether a failing test is an orphan or a real failure, it's a real failure until proven otherwise — fix the code, don't hide the test.

## Related

- `ac-emission` — how tests tag ACs and emit `(ac_uid, status, test_identifier)`. Renaming the test function or moving the file changes the `test_identifier`, which is exactly what creates orphans.
- `test-coverage` — don't declare a task done without a tagged test for every active AC. Retiring an orphan is the other half: don't leave a dead identifier pinning an AC red.
